title: Granny square — basic three rounds
subtitle: The starter motif behind every granny blanket
excerpt: The Victorian three-round granny square in DK cotton — twelve clusters of three trebles, separated by chain spaces, building outward from a magic-ring centre. The classic motif that joins into blankets, cushions, bags, and bunting. Twenty minutes per finished square once the rhythm is in the hands.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Crochet, volumes covering the 1880s–1900s — the granny square as a household motif appears in multiple Weldon's volumes alongside afghan-square assembly instructions. Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886), crochet chapter — documents the cluster-of-trebles construction that the granny square formalises. The three-round count and the colour-block convention come from twentieth-century afghan tradition; the underlying stitch structure is Victorian.

crochet.gaugeText: Each three-round granny square measures 9–10 cm across the diagonal in DK cotton on a 4 mm hook, before blocking. Blocked, the squares settle to 10 × 10 cm with crisper corners.
crochet.finishedSizeText: 9–10 cm per square unblocked; 10 × 10 cm blocked.

recipeTools.notes: For weaving in the cut ends — two per square if changing colour each round, one if working in a single colour.
For checking the square's size against the gauge target.
Optional but recommended. A foam blocking mat lets the squares set into a true 10 × 10 cm square once steam-blocked.
Rust-proof T-pins for holding the corners square during blocking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Three trebles worked into the same chain space. The repeat unit of every granny pattern — the chain space holds the cluster open, and the three trebles fan slightly to give the granny its characteristic open texture.

The gap created between two stitches by working one or more chains over the gap. The granny square's clusters sit in the chain spaces of the previous round; the chain spaces become the corners and the sides of the motif.

The chain stitches worked at the start of a row or round to bring the hook to the height of the first real stitch. A treble takes a 3-chain turning chain. In the granny square the turning chain stands in for the first treble of each round so the count stays exact.

Setting the finished shape of a crochet piece by holding a steam iron 2-3 cm above the pinned-out work without touching the iron to the fabric. The steam relaxes the yarn into the pinned shape; the piece dries flat and square. Faster than wet blocking, and the standard for cotton motifs.

content.text: The granny square is the most-made motif in crochet. Three rounds of

— separated by single chains, build outward from a closed centre. The finished square is the building block of granny blankets, granny bags, granny cushion fronts, and the ubiquitous granny bunting. This pattern is the three-round starter: a single square at about 10 × 10 cm in DK cotton, the size most older afghan patterns assume.

The pattern below is written in UK terminology. UK "treble" is US "double crochet"; UK "double crochet" does not appear here. The renderer offers a UK / US terminology toggle for readers more comfortable in the US convention. The stitch counts and structure are identical either way.

About 20 g of the centre colour and 20 g of the outer colour will make one square with a margin to spare. Mercerised cotton (Drops Paris, Scheepjes Cahlista, Yarn and Colors Must-Have) gives the cleanest stitch definition. The pattern works in any DK yarn, but cotton holds the square shape best after blocking. The exercise here is the structure; the colour choice is the reader's.
Standard DK pairing. If your tension runs loose, drop to 3.5 mm to keep the square at 10 × 10 cm; if your tension is tight, move up to 4.5 mm.
Tapestry needle, sharp scissors, soft measuring tape.
The tapestry needle weaves in the cut ends — at minimum two per square (start tail and final tail), more when changing colour at each round.
A foam tile or a folded towel and four T-pins are enough. Blocking sets the cotton fibres into a true square and is the difference between a slightly-wonky motif and a precise one.

Each three-round granny square measures 9–10 cm across the diagonal in DK cotton on a 4 mm hook, before blocking. Blocked, the squares settle to 10 × 10 cm with crisper corners. The gauge is not optional: a blanket of forty-five granny squares depends on the squares being the same size, and a single square that comes off 1 cm bigger throws every join and seam across the whole project. Make one square, measure, and adjust the hook size before committing to a full set.
the adjustable starting loop. The first round of the square is worked into the ring; the tail pulls it closed.
and as the chain spaces that separate clusters and form corners.
the main pattern stitch. Three trebles worked into a single chain space make one cluster.
joining stitch. Used to close each round into a circle and to move the hook across the work to the next start position.

. Ch 3 (counts as first treble), 2 tr into the ring, ch 2 (first corner space), [3 tr into the ring, ch 2] three more times. Sl st to the top of the beginning ch-3 to join. (12 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces.) Pull the tail to close the ring. Fasten off centre colour, leaving a 10 cm tail.

Join the outer colour with a sl st into any ch-2 corner space. Ch 3 (counts as first treble), [2 tr into the same corner space, ch 2, 3 tr into the same corner space] — the first corner is now formed. The pattern for round 2 from here: ch 1, then *(3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr) all into the next ch-2 corner space, ch 1, repeat from * twice more. Sl st to the top of the beginning ch-3 to join. (8 clusters of 3 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces, 4 ch-1 side spaces.)

Sl st across the next 2 trebles to bring the hook to the corner space. Ch 3 (counts as first treble), 2 tr into the corner space, ch 2, 3 tr into the same corner space (first corner formed). *Ch 1, 3 tr into the next ch-1 side space, ch 1, (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr) into the next ch-2 corner space*, repeat from * to * twice more. Ch 1, 3 tr into the final ch-1 side space, ch 1. Sl st to the top of the beginning ch-3 to join. (12 clusters of 3 tr, 4 ch-2 corner spaces, 8 ch-1 side spaces.)
Fasten off, leaving a 10 cm tail for weaving in. Square is complete.

Thread each tail onto the tapestry needle. Weave through three stitches in the back of the work, take a small backstitch to lock the tail, then weave another two stitches in the opposite direction. Trim the tail close to the work. With four tails (the magic-ring start, the centre-colour fasten-off, the outer-colour join, and the outer-colour fasten-off), one square needs about three minutes of finishing work — more than the crochet itself takes once the rhythm is in.

Pin the four corners to a foam blocking mat with the square stretched gently to 10 × 10 cm — measure the diagonal from corner to corner; it should be 14 cm (the diagonal of a 10 × 10 cm square). Hold a steam iron 2-3 cm above the pinned work and
for ten seconds — do not touch the iron to the fabric. Leave to dry pinned out (an hour or two in a warm room). Cotton blocks crisply and holds the square shape.

Granny squares in cotton handwash cool with wool wash or mild soap. Lay flat to dry, reshaping to the blocked dimensions if the wash distorts the corners. Machine washing on a wool cycle works for sturdy DK cotton but the cycle should be cool (30°C) and the spin gentle; the join seams between assembled squares are the failure point in machine washing, not the squares themselves.

The three-round granny is the entry point to a long line of variations. A six-round granny gives a 20 × 20 cm cushion-front-sized square; a continuous-colour granny is worked from a single colour for a more uniform finish; the solid granny replaces the chain spaces with extra trebles for a denser fabric. Once forty-five identical three-round squares stack up, a blanket-from-grannies is the natural next project — joining methods (whipstitch, slip stitch seam, or join-as-you-go) become the next thing to learn.
